full_response:
Let R = {p1 , … , pn } be the set of centres of the n routers; each router covers the unit–disk
D(pi ) = { x ∈ ℝ^2 : ‖x – pi ‖ ≤ 1 }.
For a set S ⊆ R write MIS(S) for any subset whose disks are pair-wise disjoint
(‖pi – pj ‖ ≥ 2 for every two centres in S).
Let OPT ⊆ R be a maximum such set and m = |OPT|.

Goal Produce, in polynomial time, a set A with E[|A|] ≥ m⁄4.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Random grid
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.   Pick two independent reals x0 , y0  ∼ U[0,2)               ▹  random offset  
2.   Draw vertical lines X = x0 + 2k and horizontal lines Y = y0 + 2ℓ
     (k,ℓ ∈ ℤ).  They cut the plane into congruent squares of side 2.
3.   Index every square by the integer pair (k,ℓ) of its column / row.
     Its colour is the parity pair c(k,ℓ) = (k mod 2 , ℓ mod 2) ∈ {0,1}².
     There are therefore four colours: 00, 01, 10, 11.

Because x0 , y0 are chosen from a continuum, with probability 1 no router
centre lies exactly on a grid line; ties may be broken arbitrarily.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Algorithm
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
For every colour c ∈ {00,01,10,11}

      Sc ← ∅
      For every square Q whose colour is c
             RQ  ← {pi ∈ R : pi is inside Q}
             Ic  ← an exact maximum independent subset of RQ
                       (details below)
             Sc  ← Sc ∪ Ic

Return  A = argmaxc |Sc|        ▹ keep the largest of the four sets

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Why is A interference–free?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• Within one square Q we explicitly chose Ic to be independent.  
• Consider two distinct squares Q and Q′ of the same colour.
  Their row indices differ by an even number and so do their column indices.
  Hence the x-distance between Q and Q′ is at least 2 and
  the y-distance is at least 2.
  Therefore any two points, one in Q and one in Q′, are at distance ≥ 2,
  so the corresponding disks cannot overlap.
Consequently all disks whose centres lie in ⋃Q Ic are disjoint.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
How to find Ic inside one square Q
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The diameter of Q is 2.
Any two independent routers must have centres ≥ 2 apart,
so at most four routers of OPT can lie in one such square
(indeed, partition Q into four 1×1 subsquares; a subsquare can
 contain at most one centre).
Thus |Ic | ≤ 4.

Brute force suffices:  
enumerate every subset of RQ of size ≤ 4
and pick the largest independent one.
If |RQ| = t this costs O(t^4);          (t ≤ n)
in the worst case the whole algorithm runs in O(n^4) time
and O(n) space – still polynomial.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Approximation ratio
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
For every colour c let OPTc = OPT ∩ (⋃ squares of colour c).

• For every square Q of colour c, OPT ∩ Q is an independent set inside Q,
  so |Ic | ≥ |OPT ∩ Q| by maximality of Ic.
  Summing over all squares of colour c gives |Sc| ≥ |OPTc|.

• Therefore |A| = maxc |Sc| ≥ maxc |OPTc|.

• Since the maximum of four numbers is at least their average,

        maxc |OPTc| ≥ (|OPT00| + |OPT01| + |OPT10| + |OPT11|)/4 = m/4.

Hence |A| ≥ m/4 always.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Expected performance
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Because the lower bound |A| ≥ m/4 holds for every realisation of the random
offset (and the algorithm always terminates in polynomial time),
we also have

        E[|A|]  ≥  m/4.

Thus the algorithm is a randomized polynomial-time 4-approximation.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Complexities
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
• Building the grid and classifying the n centres:      Θ(n).
• Computing the local MIS’s:                           O(n^4) time,
                                                        O(n)   space.
Both are polynomial in the input size.

\boxed{\text{A random 2\times2 grid with four colours, keep the largest colour
           class; this delivers a 1/4-approximation in expectation (and in fact
           in every run) in polynomial time.}}

Confidence: 0.55
